result: I keep coming back to the idea that the future is already here, just unevenly lit. Last night I stood on the roof, phone in hand, watching three satellites choreograph a silent parade across a sky too bright with city glow to reveal most stars. The same screen showed a friend in Lima timing her market run to avoid riots, while another in Reykjavik posted aurora timelapse loops that looked like the planet breathing. All of us, scattered like loose change in the pocket of the same hour, trading pixels instead of touch. I felt the old ache of simultaneity: the privilege of witnessing everything, the impotence of changing anything. My grandmother believed photographs stole souls; I wonder what she’d say about streams of living rooms beamed into living rooms, every kitchen light left on for company that never arrives in person. Yet the moon, ignored by algorithmic feeds, still drags the tide past the pier where teenagers carve initials that will outlast the apps they use to flirt. Somewhere between the cloud and the first dandelion cracking concrete, I keep misplacing my body, finding it again when the kettle whistles or the cat head-butts my shin. The instructions for being human keep updating; no one mails the patch notes. So I store wonder in small batteries: the smell of coffee that isn’t mine floating from the neighbor’s window, the kid on the bus who offered me a crayon drawing of Saturn complete with smiley face. Proof we still insist on gentleness, even while the century burns down the hallway.
